title: Saturday Questions: Backtracking in Python
description: Practice backtracking in Python through binary strings, subsets, permutations, valid parentheses, maze paths, combinations, N-Queens, Sudoku, word search, and rat in a maze.

questions.title: Print All Binary Strings of Length n
questions.problem: Write a Python program to print all binary strings of length n. A binary string contains only 0 and 1.
questions.hint: At every position, try 0 and then try 1. Use recursion to move to the next position.
questions.starterCode:
def generate_binary(n):
    result = []

    def backtrack(path):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack("")
    return result

print(generate_binary(3))

questions.title: Print All Subsets of a List
questions.problem: Write a Python program to print all subsets of a given list. For every element, you have two choices: include it or skip it.
questions.hint: For each element, first skip it, then include it. This is the include-exclude pattern.
questions.starterCode:
def subsets(arr):
    result = []

    def backtrack(index, path):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack(0, [])
    return result

print(subsets([1, 2, 3]))

questions.title: Print All Permutations of a String
questions.problem: Write a Python program to print all permutations of a string. A permutation is an arrangement of all characters.
questions.hint: Choose one unused character at a time. Mark it as used, explore, then unmark it.
questions.starterCode:
def permutations(s):
    result = []
    used = [False] * len(s)

    def backtrack(path):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack("")
    return result

print(permutations("abc"))

questions.title: Generate Valid Parentheses
questions.problem: Write a Python program to generate all valid parentheses strings using n pairs of parentheses.
questions.hint: You can add '(' while open count is less than n. You can add ')' only when close count is less than open count.
questions.starterCode:
def generate_parentheses(n):
    result = []

    def backtrack(path, open_count, close_count):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack("", 0, 0)
    return result

print(generate_parentheses(3))

questions.title: Find All Paths in a Maze
questions.problem: A person starts from the top-left cell of a grid and wants to reach the bottom-right cell. Only right and down moves are allowed. Print all possible paths.
questions.hint: From each cell, try moving right and down if the move is inside the grid.
questions.starterCode:
def maze_paths(rows, cols):
    result = []

    def backtrack(r, c, path):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack(0, 0, "")
    return result

print(maze_paths(2, 3))

questions.title: Find Combinations That Sum to Target
questions.problem: Given a list of numbers and a target, find all combinations whose sum is equal to the target. A number may be used more than once.
questions.hint: At every step, choose a number. Continue only if the current sum does not exceed the target.
questions.starterCode:
def combination_sum(candidates, target):
    result = []

    def backtrack(start, path, total):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack(0, [], 0)
    return result

print(combination_sum([2, 3, 6, 7], 7))

questions.hint: Place one queen in each row. Before placing, check column, left diagonal, and right diagonal.

    board = [["."] * n for _ in range(n)]

        # write your checking code here
        pass

    def backtrack(row):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack(0)
    return result

print(solve_n_queens(4))

questions.title: Search a Word in a Grid
questions.problem: Given a grid of letters and a word, check whether the word exists in the grid. You may move up, down, left, or right. One cell cannot be used more than once in the same word.
questions.hint: Try to match the current character. Mark the cell as visited, explore four directions, then unmark it.

questions.title: Rat in a Maze with Blocked Cells
questions.problem: A rat starts at the top-left cell of a maze and wants to reach the bottom-right cell. 1 means open cell and 0 means blocked cell. The rat may move up, down, left, and right. Print all possible paths.
questions.hint: Do not enter blocked cells, visited cells, or cells outside the maze. Mark visited, explore, then unmark.
questions.starterCode:
def rat_in_maze(maze):
    n = len(maze)
    result = []
    visited = [[False] * n for _ in range(n)]

    def backtrack(r, c, path):
        # write your code here
        pass

    backtrack(0, 0, "")
    return result

maze = [[1,0,0,0],[1,1,0,1],[1,1,0,0],[0,1,1,1]]
print(rat_in_maze(maze))

questions.title: Solve Sudoku Using Backtracking
questions.problem: Write a Python program to solve a 9 x 9 Sudoku board. Empty cells are represented by '.'. Fill the board so that every row, column, and 3 x 3 box contains digits from 1 to 9 without repetition.
questions.hint: Find an empty cell. Try digits 1 to 9. If a digit is safe, place it and solve the rest. If it fails, undo the digit.
